metadata.title: Standing Up for Sweden? The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration Facebook Group

abstract: Facebook has faced growing criticism regarding its handling of hateful user-generated content (UGC) with research revealing how the platform can foster both covert and overt racism. This research has tended to focus on racist content while relying on abstract references to the general logics of social media platforms. In this article we consider how Facebook shapes the production of racist discourse in more concrete ways by integrating a concern for the platform's architectures and affordances within a broader analysis of the immigration-related discussions of a large Swedish Facebook group. We combine a quantitative topic modeling of a large data set of the group's UGC with a qualitative critical discourse analysis (CDA) of a sample of that data set. Our findings show how Facebook enables and influences various discursive strategies of identification and persuasion-within which covert and overt racist discourses are embedded-through processes of cybertyping, role-playing, crowdsourcing and (counter-)reaction.

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
paragraphs: Facebook continues to find itself at the centre of controversy. From live streaming scandals to accusations that it spreads political misinformation and, as the recent Cambridge Analytica debacle illustrates, that it fails to protect user data, the social media company's claims to neutrality have been increasingly questioned. Criticism has been particularly marked regarding its handling of hateful usergenerated content (UGC) including that which is racially motivated. Although Facebook's community standards state that groups and individuals promoting hate against people based on, among other factors, their race, ethnicity and nationality are "not allowed a presence" on its platform (Facebook, 2018), numerous international examples suggest that in practice the company is struggling to consistently maintain these standards. In Sweden this can be illustrated through reference to the case of the Stå Upp För Sverige [Stand up for Sweden] (SUFS) Facebook group.

SUFS was created as Stå upp för Peter Springare [Stand up for Peter Springare] on 5 February 2017 to support Peter Springare, a Swedish police officer, who two days earlier posted a statement on his private Facebook page, claiming that immigrants were overrepresented within Swedish crime statistics and their status as such was subject to a governmental cover-up. His post was shared over 18,000 times and received over 6,000 comments in the following week (Gustafson, 2017). Springare was subsequently reported to the police on charges of hate speech but was also celebrated by many, including the 100,000 members that joined the Facebook group in its first three days (Ohlsén Pettersson, 2017). At its peak, the group was reported to have around 220,000 members and after eighteen months, at the time of publication, still had approximately 169,000 members, despite the widespread accusations of racism that led Springare to ask people to leave the group in April 2017 and subsequently led to its renaming.

Since then SUFS has been described as a "bizarre hate cult" by a Swedish governmental minister, labeled as the country's "largest hate group" by the mainstream press, and, more recently, academically scrutinized as an online forum of the radical right (Hansson, 2017;Pfriem, 2017;Törnberg & Wahlström, 2018). However, by its own description and rules, SUFS forbids racism. We aim to establish how racism contributes to the group's discussion of immigration and to consider how the architectures and affordances of Facebook's platform allow it to do so. Through a topic modeling of SUFS's UGC and a critical discourse analysis (CDA) of specific posts, our efforts were guided by the following questions:
• How is immigration discussed in the group? • How and in what forms does racism contribute to the discussion of immigration?

• How does Facebook enable and influence this racism? Before outlining our methodological and ethical approach, and presenting our analysis, we situate these efforts with regards to the literature on digital hate and theories of discourse and racism.

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
paragraphs: Digital hate takes many forms, but the literature related to racial, ethnic, religious and national distinctions is most relevant to our research. Early studies of what was conceived as "cyber racism" showed how the Internet created a new context for the expression of established forms of racism while also facilitating new types of racist culture (Back, 2002;Daniels, 2012;Nakamura, 2002;2008). It enabled groups and individuals to spread overtly racist content and create "their own cyber-subcultures and alternative public spheres" (Atton, 2006, p. 574), with websites functioning as digital "enclaves" of hate within "a largely autonomous, although not hermetic, racist Net-world" (Back, 2002, p. 633). Websites also created a means to influence more mainstream views by, for example, "cloaking" their racist agendas behind feigned legitimacy and concealed authorship (Daniels, 2009).

The rise of social media has created new digital pathways by which hate can be laundered into information, allowing racism's further mainstreaming (Klein, 2012). Reflecting this, there has been an upturn in research into racism (and its denial) on different social media platforms (e.g., Arnstad, 2015;Awan, 2014;Ekman, 2014;Horsti, 2017;Sharma & Brooker, 2016;Törnberg & Törnberg, 2016). Facebook has featured prominently within this research, often investigated via its pages and groups, including those characterized by far-right, anti-Semitic, Islamophobic, or anti-immigration views (e.g., Ben-David & Matamoros-Fernandez, 2016;Burke & Goodman, 2012;Oboler, 2008). These studies have shown how Facebook can foster both overt and covert racist content 1 while others have shown its use can increase the acceptance of racism (Rauch & Schanz, 2013). Facebook groups like SUFS therefore are not impermeable enclaves, "filter bubbles" or "echo chambers," they also act as "gateways" to wider digitally-networked audiences (Klein, 2012;Törnberg & Wahlström, 2018).

Much of the aforementioned research has relied on discourse analyses of racist UGC. However, when describing the mechanisms by which social media enables and influences the production of racist discourse, many have tended to rely on abstract references to social media's general logics (see Van Dijk & Poell, 2013). One exception is Matamoros-Fernández's (2017) research on "platformed racism"-the racism facilitated by social media's for-profit modes of governance and ability to manufacture and amplify certain messages. But, as Matamoros-Fernández and others suggest, more research that attends to the concrete means by which the architectures and affordances of social media platforms like Facebook shape the production of racism in different national settings is needed (Matamoros-Fernández, 2017;Ben-David & Matamoros-Fernandez, 2016). Social media architectures are "packages of technological characteristics" that, in influencing how different platforms are used to communicate, shape UGC (Freelon, 2015, p. 776). They can include "normative interface" features like functions, buttons, add-ons, options and the ability to report or filter-out offensive UGC (Cirucci, 2017). Affordances meanwhile are the broader limitations and possibilities that these architectures afford users and UGC while also indicating the "social characteristics of human-technology interaction" (Nagy & Neff, 2015, p. 7). boyd, for example, discusses how social media's different architectural features cause four structural affordances: persistence, visibility, spreadability, and searchability (2011). These architectures and affordances influence the production and interpretation of discourse and in turn the "construction, mediation, and materialization of power and social relationships" (Freelon, 2015;Nagy & Neff, 2015, p. 4). Discourse analysis thus remains relevant to understanding how social media platforms like Facebook can foster racism, because especially in its critical variants, it can account for how these platforms, their architectures and affordances, serve as meso-level discursive bridges between the micro-level of UGC and the macro-level of society.

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
paragraphs: When approached critically, discourse-spoken and written language-is considered both socially constitutive and conditioned, a form of "social practice" implying the relationship between any single discursive event and its surrounding situations, institutions and social structures (Wodak & Meyer, 2009). Discourse exists across the micro-level of the situation-texts of spoken or written language; the meso-level of the institution-interactions of discursive practice through which texts are produced and interpreted; and the macro-level of society-the context of wider sociocultural practices that condition the production and interpretation of texts (Fairclough, 1989). Given that discourse can have hegemonic and ideological effects by contributing to the (re)production of uneven power relations, its critical analysis illuminates social phenomena like racism (Fairclough & Wodak, 1997).

Considerable scholarly energy has been spent within the field of CDA investigating contemporary forms of racism. The forms of racism analyzed in this way are often those which are more coded, covert, and deniable rather than the older, more self-evident pseudoscientific and systematic forms of racism-such as race biology and slavery-or the sorts of overtly racist violence perpetrated by extremist groups. In other words, cultural rather than biological forms of racism. Although covert racism can be associated with intentionally-veiled extremist, nationalist and populist sentiments, it can also result from broader institutionalized modes of racism enacted through social and political policy. The study of concealed forms of racism is even more important given the belief that racism is a thing of the past. The idea of post-racial societies, however, also discursively veils continuing racism, incorporating and expanding the idea of cultural racism as "raceless racism" (Goldberg, 2015). Raceless racism is thus discursively instantiated at a time when racism is both prohibited and claimed to be non-existent (Valluvan, 2016). Racism denial on a more individual level has a deeper lineage and along with the reliance on discourses that perpetuate "us versus them" designations links cultural and raceless racism (Van Dijk, 1992, 2000). Both create and target cultural and non-racial race categories like "immigrants" while primarily discriminating against non-white people through their Othering and the idea of "whiteness": the multi-dimensional and structural conditions that elevate white people over others through their racialization (DiAngelo, 2011;Valluvan, 2016). This is partly achieved through the racist discursive strategies described by Wodak and Reisigl (2001). These relate to the manner by which in-and out-groups are discursively produced, more or less intentionally, in pursuit of different social, political, psychological or linguistic goals. They include nomination or referential strategies that construct and label in-and out-group members. Overlapping with these, predicational strategies present in-groups positively and out-groups, negatively. These strategies are justified by argumentation strategies while perspectivation strategies allow the producer of the discourse to position themselves in relation to the discourse and frame their points of view. Finally, intensification or mitigation strategies are used in order to reinforce or moderate racist propositions.

The discursive study of racism has also foregrounded the role of political and media elites because of their influence over public discourse and the role that the broadcast and printed press plays as a major source of information (Van Dijk, 2000;2015). Racism in this context has been associated with topics like immigration, which although neutral often has its negative dimensions over-emphasized in order to present immigrants, as problematic, threatening or deviant (Van Dijk, 2000). Although elites are influential in contemporary racism's reproduction, racism is also created through the interplay of "public, semi-public, and private fields of discourse" (Wodak & Reisigl, 1999, p. 186). The interplay of these fields is inherent in social media platforms like Facebook because the networked publics that they facilitate involve collapsed social contexts that blur distinctions between publicness and privacy (boyd, 2011). These platforms have thus risen to challenge the control that mainstream political and media elites traditionally exercised over public discourse. But while the decentralized, connective and crowdsourced principles of social media platforms complicate the idea of a clearly identifiable elite (and indeed mainstream), with greater power over the production of public discourse, they do not altogether preclude such power from being exerted by different actors, including so-called "social media influencers", social media companies and the platforms themselves.

Although covert racism can be associated with intentionally-veiled extremist, nationalist and populist sentiments, it can also result from broader institutionalized modes of racism enacted through social and political policy. The study of concealed forms of racism is even more important given the belief that racism is a thing of the past. The idea of post-racial societies, however, also discursively veils continuing racism, incorporating and expanding the idea of cultural racism as "raceless racism" (Goldberg, 2015). Raceless racism is thus discursively instantiated at a time when racism is both prohibited and claimed to be non-existent (Valluvan, 2016). Racism denial on a more individual level has a deeper lineage and along with the reliance on discourses that perpetuate "us versus them" designations links cultural and raceless racism (Van Dijk, 1992, 2000). Both create and target cultural and non-racial race categories like "immigrants" while primarily discriminating against non-white people through their Othering and the idea of "whiteness": the multi-dimensional and structural conditions that elevate white people over others through their racialization (DiAngelo, 2011;Valluvan, 2016). This is partly achieved through the racist discursive strategies described by Wodak and Reisigl (2001). These relate to the manner by which in-and out-groups are discursively produced, more or less intentionally, in pursuit of different social, political, psychological or linguistic goals. They include nomination or referential strategies that construct and label in-and out-group members. Overlapping with these, predicational strategies present in-groups positively and out-groups, negatively. These strategies are justified by argumentation strategies while perspectivation strategies allow the producer of the discourse to position themselves in relation to the discourse and frame their points of view. Finally, intensification or mitigation strategies are used in order to reinforce or moderate racist propositions.

The discursive study of racism has also foregrounded the role of political and media elites because of their influence over public discourse and the role that the broadcast and printed press plays as a major source of information (Van Dijk, 2000;2015). Racism in this context has been associated with topics like immigration, which although neutral often has its negative dimensions over-emphasized in order to present immigrants, as problematic, threatening or deviant (Van Dijk, 2000). Although elites are influential in contemporary racism's reproduction, racism is also created through the interplay of "public, semi-public, and private fields of discourse" (Wodak & Reisigl, 1999, p. 186). The interplay of these fields is inherent in social media platforms like Facebook because the networked publics that they facilitate involve collapsed social contexts that blur distinctions between publicness and privacy (boyd, 2011). These platforms have thus risen to challenge the control that mainstream political and media elites traditionally exercised over public discourse. But while the decentralized, connective and crowdsourced principles of social media platforms complicate the idea of a clearly identifiable elite (and indeed mainstream), with greater power over the production of public discourse, they do not altogether preclude such power from being exerted by different actors, including so-called "social media influencers", social media companies and the platforms themselves.
sections.preview_head: When approached critically, discourse-spoken and written language-is considered both socially constitutive and conditioned, a form of "social practice" implying the relationship between any single discursive event and its surrounding situations, institutions and social structures (Wodak & Meyer, 2009). Discourse exists across the micro-level of the situation-texts of spoken or written language; the meso-level of the institution-interactions of discursive practice through which texts are produced and interpreted; and the macro-level of society-the context of wider sociocultural practices that condition the production and interpretation of texts (Fairclough, 1989). Given that discourse can have hegemonic and ideological effects by contributing to the (re)production of uneven power relations, its critical analysis illuminates social phenomena like racism (Fairclough & Wodak, 1997).

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
paragraphs: Our analysis of SUFS relied on the automated, quantitative study of a large data set of its UGC followed by the manual, qualitative examination of a sample of that content. This ensured our findings were representative and allowed the analysis of both the broader patterns and empirical details of SUFS's content (see Baker & Levon, 2015;Baker, 2006). It relied, in the first instance on the topic modeling of 59,489 SUFS posts and 1,754,425 associated comments from the page's creation on 5 February 2017 to 29 September 2017, gathered on 30 September 2017 using open-access python code (Freelon, 2017). Identical posts and their comments were grouped creating 50,199 documents. Those smaller than 1KB were discarded because the statistical model by which topic modeling clusters words into topics based on their probable distribution over a data set works best for larger documents (Blei, 2012a). A topic model was run on the 23,939 remaining documents using the Machine Learning for Language Toolkit (MALLET) (McCallum, 2002). Commonly occurring Swedish 'stop words' (including, for example, prepositions) were removed from the data set. The data set was not stemmed before the topic modeling in order to avoid the incorrect conflation of words and because this procedure does not necessarily improve the coherency of topics (Schofield & Mimno, 2016). Topic modeling inductively defines a predetermined number of topics based only on the data set and assumes that a percentage of every topic can be found in each document (Blei, 2012b).

Following standard procedure, the number of predetermined topics was set using a trial and error approach based on the coherency of the outputted topics. Statistical means of ensuring topic coherency were not applied because evidence suggests these are outperformed by more interactive "humanin-the-loop methods" (Chang, Boyd-Graber, Gerrish, Wang, & Blei, 2009;Lee et al., 2017). Subsequently, we used MALLET to model 50 topics, each with its own number, a list of most representative words called a "topic key," and a "topic weight" indicating its distribution over the entire data set. Because of SUFS's proclaimed interest in immigration, the topics where the stem of the Swedish word for immigration/immigrant (invandr*) appeared earlier in the topic key, namely in the topic's 100 most representative words, were chosen for further analysis. This narrowed our analysis to 10 topics, selected not in order to understand the discourses of the group discussion as a whole but, according to our research questions, those specifically related to immigration (Table 1). Topic modeling was thus used to both sample and analyze SUFS's UGC.

A surface reading of the 10 topics' 100 most representative words and the five documents with the greatest percentage proportion of each topic (regardless of whether they contained the search stem) informed their naming and description (Table 2). The topic names and descriptions were refined following a CDA of a sub-sample of 50 documents. These 50 documents were composed of the five documents containing the search stem with the greatest percentage proportion of each of the 10 topics. These documents were not necessarily the most characteristic of the 10 topics but they were always among each topic's top 20 such documents. Likewise, the posts and comments they featured were not necessarily the most popular nor the most explicitly racist. Because of identical posts these 50 documents were composed of 54 posts and 1171 comments. The selection of one document was skewed because it contained five identical posts, making it indicate a certain topic more strongly. However, the grouping of identical posts intentionally acknowledged the power of repeated discursive acts. Documents were thus treated as discussions in which viewpoints could be reiterated to various extents. These discussions were not only studied in textual form but also via the platform's user interface. 2 This also allowed the CDA of replies to comments and highlighted how Facebook shaped the production and interpretation of racist discourses. Further analytical emphasis was placed on a discourse's meso-level by identifying the racist discursive strategies described by Wodak and Reisigl by posing their suggested questions to the CDA sample:

(1) How are persons named and referred to linguistically? (2) Which traits, characteristics, qualities, and features are attributed to them? (3) By means of which arguments and argumentation schemes do specific persons or social groups try to justify and legitimate the exclusion, discrimination, suppression, and exploitation of others? (4) From which perspective or point of view are these nominations, attributions, and arguments expressed? (5) Are the respective discriminating utterances articulated overtly, are they even intensified, or are they mitigated?" (2001, p. 385) We considered these strategies under two broader categories: strategies of identification (nomination or referential, predicational and perspectivation strategies) and strategies of persuasion (argumentation, intensification or mitigation strategies). Table 2 Continued Topic number Topic name Topic description Topic weight over entire data set Topic range across the five documents chosen for CDA (%) Documents chosen for CDA are among the n documents most characteristic of the topic 31 Accommodation Focuses on issues of accommodation, and includes the argument that immigrants receive preferential treatment with regards to housing. .07886 43.25-56.06 9 39 Definitions of Racism Focuses on the definition of racism and includes the argument that white people are the greatest victims of racism. .12528 46.84-54.93 7 41 Truth Focuses on establishing the truth regarding immigration and includes the argument that the mainstream political and media establishment have conspired to cover-up this truth. .11597 45.61-58.87 14 43 Employment Focuses on the consequences of immigration for employment levels and includes the argument that immigrants both fill jobs and are not willing to work. .12626 41.29-51.55 15 46 Party Politics Focuses on the immigration policies of Sweden's mainstream political parties and in particular critiques the Centre Party (Centerpartiet) and its leader Annie Lööf. .06336 40.24-65.69 11

We maintained a self-critical ethical stance and decision-making process throughout the study (see Markham & Buchanan, 2012). This meant acknowledging that our research might inadvertently contribute to the normalization of racism by implicitly distinguishing between "legitimate" views on immigration and expressions of racism, potentially reinforcing the conditions that encourage the latter to be disguised as the former. Our attempts to reveal the discursive power of Facebook also relied in places on the term "platform," a term that reinforces the social media company's claim to neutrality (Gillespie, 2010). For ethical reasons and through acts of translation we have endeavored to ensure-to the greatest extent possible-the anonymity of the authors of the analyzed Facebook posts, comments and replies. This is important because the public profile and scale of SUFS precluded its effective anonymization, and because its UGC could be expected, in certain circumstances, to constitute illegal hate speech meaning that its circulation beyond its intended context might expose users to harm (see Williams, Burnap, & Sloan, 2017). It is harder to guarantee anonymity for the group's creator, administrators (admins) and moderators. The former considers themselves a public figure and can thus be scrutinized as such. Many of those who served as admins and moderators during the period of SUFS activity analyzed are no longer active in the same capacity.

Due to these and other complexities it is insufficient to suggest that a Facebook group's public status limits ethical considerations only to ensuring the anonymity of its contributors (Zimmer, 2010). The incoherency of Facebook groups in general must be stressed and the variated motivations of their admins, moderators, members, contributors and users acknowledged. While it can be argued convincingly that membership acts as an endorsement of SUFS's general discussion of immigration and, in turn, support for any racist discourse that contributes to that discussion, this oversimplifies the manner by which the group serves as both an enclave and gateway. To generalize about a Facebook group like SUFS with a large fluctuating membership is ethically problematic, not least because not all of its contributors are equally active. The main data set of 1,813,914 SUFS posts and comments was authored by 73,928 Facebook users with the individual share of activity reflecting the "long-tail" pattern of social media use. The most active 3,853 users (circa 5% of the total) were responsible for 1,094,767 posts and comments (circa 60% of the data set). The 110 most active users (circa .15% of the total) exerted particular quantitative influence over SUFS's discussion in being responsible for 169,286 posts and comments (circa 9% of the data set). These measures indicate the presence of social media influencers in the group and should be kept in mind when considering the preceding analysis. So too should those architectures and affordances that mean Facebook users beyond SUFS members can write posts, comments and replies in the group, and that some members may have joined to oppose or moderate SUFS, while others may have stopped using Facebook, forgotten about their membership, or been admitted to the group automatically. Others still may represent "fake" accounts.

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
paragraphs: Seven of the 10 immigration-related topics reflected more specific discussions pertaining to economy, criminality, deportation, international immigration, accommodation, employment and party politics while three reflected more general debates about freedom of speech, definitions of racism and truth. With all 50 topics constituting each document to varying extents, a topic's weight across the main data set and its percentage range across its five CDA documents indicates the most salient topics within the group's discussion of immigration (Table 2). Some topics were concentrated in certain documents that discuss immigration. The economy, criminality and deportation topics, for example, collectively account for between 64.77% and 96.67% of their CDA documents. The other seven topics were more diffuse within the CDA sample, collectively ranging between 40.24% and 65.69%. Some of these topics were significant when it came to defining the data set overall as the freedom of speech topic clearly indicates. Its high weight over the entire data set made it not only the most probable of the 10 immigration-related topics but of all 50 topics modeled (Table 1). This indicates that the topic characterized and connected the entire data set including the CDA sample. In comparison, the other nine topics, and in particular the party politics, accommodation and international immigration topics, were more peripheral across the entire data set, indicating their restriction to particular immigrationrelated documents and discussions.

All of the CDA documents contained anti-immigration discourses that relied on generic, preexisting interpretations of immigration as negatively affecting the economy, increasing crime, and causing competition for jobs and homes in Sweden. Confirming evidence from other contexts, these discourses often relied on "us versus them" designations grounded in the perceived threat that immigrants pose to non-immigrants and their national culture and heritage (Van Dijk, 2000). Reflecting these oppositions, "criminals," "refugees," "Sweden," "Swede" and "Swedish" were among the words most prominently shared between the 10 immigration-related topics, featuring in the most representative 100 words of five or more topics (Figure 1).

In general, the discussions that constituted these topics involved a tendency to contrast nostalgic interpretations of Sweden's past with dystopic diagnoses and forecasts of the country's present and future. 3 Users expressed nostalgia for the time before immigration occasionally through references to, among other periods, the Viking age and the 1940s and 1950s. That these "pasts" are predominantly white-and male-dominated reflects the historical links between ideas of Swedishness, whiteness and masculinity (see Mattsson, 2005). The nostalgia at play within SUFS was clearly of a restorative character-relying on the "return to national symbols and myths" (Boym, 2001, p. 41). It was a nostalgia that used conspiracy theory and intimate cultural knowledge to differentiate between in-and outgroups, a nostalgia that was particularly attractive to the disenfranchised who seek scapegoats for their misfortunes (p. 41-43), and a nostalgia that can also be found in the rhetoric of the Sweden Democrats (SD), the country's largest far-right political party. Seven of the 10 immigration-related topics reflected more specific discussions pertaining to economy, criminality, deportation, international immigration, accommodation, employment and party politics while three reflected more general debates about freedom of speech, definitions of racism and truth. With all 50 topics constituting each document to varying extents, a topic's weight across the main data set and its percentage range across its five CDA documents indicates the most salient topics within the group's discussion of immigration (Table 2). Some topics were concentrated in certain documents that discuss immigration. The economy, criminality and deportation topics, for example, collectively account for between 64.77% and 96.67% of their CDA documents. The other seven topics were more diffuse within the CDA sample, collectively ranging between 40.24% and 65.69%. Some of these topics were significant when it came to defining the data set overall as the freedom of speech topic clearly indicates. Its high weight over the entire data set made it not only the most probable of the 10 immigration-related topics but of all 50 topics modeled (Table 1). This indicates that the topic characterized and connected the entire data set including the CDA sample. In comparison, the other nine topics, and in particular the party politics, accommodation and international immigration topics, were more peripheral across the entire data set, indicating their restriction to particular immigrationrelated documents and discussions.

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
sections.header_raw: Cybertypes and role-playing: strategies of identification
sections.header_norm: cybertypes and role playing strategies of identification
paragraphs: Three primary "cybertypes"-collaboratively constructed and digitally-enabled stereotypes that "stabilize a sense of white self and identity" (Nakamura, 2002, p. 5)-were foregrounded in the CDA sample: immigrants, politically correct (PC) elites and Swedes. These reflected internationally resonant tropes "already cued through broader, and increasingly digital, global mediations" (Valluvan, 2016(Valluvan, , p. 2247)). Created through the accumulative, fragmentary and often contradictory nomination, referential and predicational strategies of individual Facebook users, these cybertypes were not static. Their discursive construction involved complex overlapping boundaries rather than simple binaries of "us versus them" (see Mattsson, 2005). Although detailed separately below, they were discussed in a relational manner, defined and meaningful only with respect to one another. These cybertypes supported digital role-playing, revealing that although social media has generally restricted the Internet's earlier forms of identity tourism (see Nakamura, 2002), Facebook still allows users to adopt fluid subject roles. SUFS's contributors, for instance, often used one or a combination of three main perspectivation strategies to cast themselves in the role of truth-teller, victim and realist.

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
sections.header_raw: Nomination, referential, and predicational strategies Immigrants
sections.header_norm: nomination referential and predicational strategies immigrants
paragraphs: Immigrants were generally categorized as a homogenous group of illiterate, unskilled and work-shy, benefit cheats and criminals who place economic strain on Sweden because they undeservedly require social welfare, take-up accommodation and "steal jobs from unemployed ethnic Swedes." They were often assumed to be non-European, non-white, and equated to Muslims. Elsewhere in SUFS, however, some statements distinguished between criminal and non-criminal immigrants and "real refugees," Figure 1 The connections between topics in terms of their 100 most representative words.

who were defined as willing to be civilized by "Swedish values." Other refugees were accused of deceitfully migrating to Sweden under the guise of seeking asylum, including young men considered to have taken advantage of regulations surrounding unaccompanied child refugees, derogatorily labeled "bearded children." Echoing earlier precedents immigrants were depicted passively except in negative situations when they received responsibility and blame (see Van Dijk, 2000). They were, for example, contradictorily depicted as a passive cargo, imported and exported by the Swedish government for economic gain. They were also accused of bringing "new" crimes to Sweden, nostalgically denying that such crimes existed before immigration and denying their perpetration by non-immigrants -"they are like rats and only men come here. How will things be in the future with cases of new rape?" Some of the most overt examples of racism thus involved immigrants' dehumanization and animalization (Goldberg, 2015). Mirroring findings about the construction of other post-race racial categories, immigrants were depicted as both warlike "invading hordes" and dirty "parasites" and "animals," and, in one case, were accused of infesting their temporary housing with Ebola and bedbugs (Horsti, 2017;Valluvan, 2016).

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
paragraphs: Besides immigrants, PC elites and their establishments were presented as responsible for Sweden's perceived dystopic circumstances. Users predicted that the PC establishment's "ridiculous" approach to immigration will lead to Sweden's Islamization and transformation into a "developing country within a generation"-a viewpoint that was supported by ironic and covertly racist references to the country as "Absurdistan" and "Swedenstan." The Swedish government and its agencies; the seven parliamentary political parties besides the SD; the national and international mainstream media; and the European Union (EU) were identified as PC establishments. Charged with ineptitude, inadequately shouldering responsibility for immigration, declining to represent the interests of "real Swedes" and over-prioritizing the needs of immigrants, the Swedish PC establishment recurrently featured in conspiracy theories that claimed the supposed failures of the country's recent immigration policies have been purposefully hidden from the wider public. The EU meanwhile, was charged with undermining Sweden's sovereignty and the ability of "native Swedes" to "decide who gets to live in Sweden." Some SUFS users felt targeted by the mainstream press and abandoned and betrayed by mainstream politicians. In extremis, this led to aggressive counterattacks but also the belief that it would be easier to be an immigrant. For example, one user suggested that sunless tanning creams increase the chance of receiving welfare because the Swedish authorities favour the "dark kind." This reveals how whiteness lies behind the racism evident in SUFS, as does the branding of the predominantly white PC establishment as traitors because of their challenge to white solidarity (see DiAngelo, 2011).

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
paragraphs: The idea of "the Swede" regularly conveyed an imagined sense of national homogeneity, unity and community (see Anderson, 1991). As alluded to, however, different categories of Swede were indicated as "real," "native" and "ethnic" or alternatively, "new," creating hierarchies of Swedishness. So, although second-generation immigrants with Swedish citizenship were welcomed by some users, others argued that Swedish citizenship alone does not make a person Swedish. For instance, it was considered absurd that after five years of residency, anyone can become Swedish in the eyes of the law, and that second-generation immigrants are not separated from "real Swedes" in crime statistics. "Real Swedes" can be taken to covertly mean white Swedes, especially as in SUFS skin colour was primarily signaled when not white. "New Swede" was thus mostly used to indicate non-white Swedish citizens with an immigration background. Swedishness also relied on other factors with the default positive and superior portrayal of "real Swedes" linked to the assumption that they universally pay their taxes, abide by the law and only claim social benefits legitimately. In contrast, "new Swedes" and immigrants must prove they abide by and contribute to Swedish law and society. They must earn their Swedishness as statements like "WE, the entire Swedish people including law-abiding immigrants (…)" exemplify. Despite these general tendencies it was difficult to discern a comprehensive consensus regarding Swedishness and the boundaries between these types of Swede. Subsequently contradictions in opinion were rife. Additional complexities arise from the manner by which users regularly distinguished themselves from a wider population of "naïve Swedes" who are either too cowardly to resist or have been deceived by PC elites.

sections.preview_head: The idea of "the Swede" regularly conveyed an imagined sense of national homogeneity, unity and community (see Anderson, 1991). As alluded to, however, different categories of Swede were indicated as "real," "native" and "ethnic" or alternatively, "new," creating hierarchies of Swedishness. So, although second-generation immigrants with Swedish citizenship were welcomed by some users, others argued that Swedish citizenship alone does not make a person Swedish.
sections.preview_tail: Despite these general tendencies it was difficult to discern a comprehensive consensus regarding Swedishness and the boundaries between these types of Swede. Subsequently contradictions in opinion were rife. Additional complexities arise from the manner by which users regularly distinguished themselves from a wider population of "naïve Swedes" who are either too cowardly to resist or have been deceived by PC elites.

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
paragraphs: Following Springare, the SD and others, many of SUFS's users viewed themselves as speaking a commonly-known truth which few are brave enough to share publicly-that the Swedish government's immigration policy has failed. In speaking their truth many activated their right to free speech in order to legitimize their anti-immigration opinions. From this perspective political correctness was judged to force people into silence or self-censorship by functioning as a "wet blanket" over the immigration debate-a metaphor which reveals the implicit comprehension of immigration as a fire that will soon be out of control. In their efforts to share their truth, many in the group saw themselves as resisting a corridor of PC opinion that suppresses their views (Törnberg & Wahlström, 2018). One contributor struggled to understand how some equated SUFS membership with right-wing extremism and instead framed their participation as supporting those who speak of "a reality" that they had "known for a long time to be correct." Besides indicating more institutionalized forms of racism this also highlights how SUFS users perceived themselves as victims of false accusations alongside other injustices, perpetuated not only by the PC elite but also by immigrants.

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
paragraphs: Despite the appearance of both explicit and implicit examples of group users' assumed superiority over immigrants, indicating how the nomination of the "real Swede" relies on the overtly racist trope of white supremacy, users also regularly perceived themselves to be victims in ways symptomatic of covert racism (see Atton, 2006). The severity of this assumed victimhood varied from the white fragility that results from the subtlest interruptions of white racial privilege and comfort (DiAngelo, 2011), to accusations of systematic white genocide. "Real Swedes" were viewed as victimized by a traitorous PC establishment and the immigrants to whom they supposedly grant unfair and undeserved advantage. Certain categories of "real Swede" gained particular attention as victims including those young people who cannot leave their parent's homes due to the high demand for accommodation caused by immigration and the homeless, elderly and unemployed people who face unfair competition from immigrants not only for accommodation and jobs but social welfare in general. More extreme uses of the victim perspective by group contributors revolved around claims of reverse discrimination and racism (see Van Dijk, 1992) and global conspiratorial plans to carry out "white genocide."

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
paragraphs: While less prominent than the other perspectivation strategies, certain SUFS contributors cast themselves as realists when expressing their anti-immigration viewpoints. Humanitarian arguments for supporting immigration, and in particular the arrival in Sweden of asylum seekers and refugees, were outweighed by more utilitarian interpretations that immigration and universal social welfare are incompatible with one another, once again mirroring the SD's political rhetoric (see Hellström, Nilsson, & Stoltz, 2012). One contributor pondered: "How do you help the most? That is the question compassion forces us to ask." So, while some users acknowledged that certain refugees and asylum seekers require help, they also argued this help should not be provided at the expense of the social welfare of "real Swedes." By extension some argued that the generosity of the Swedish government increases immigration thus depriving immigrants' home countries of their workforce and leaving immigrants in states of "non-productive welfare dependency" in Sweden. Following the utilitarian logic of the greatest good for the greatest number, while hinting towards an implicit white supremacist worldview, others argued that immigration should be discouraged and "repatriation" prioritized so that immigrants might rebuild their home countries based on their experiences of Sweden.

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
sections.header_norm: crowdsourcing and counter reactions strategies of persuasion
paragraphs: The CDA also revealed how the networked affordances of Facebook influenced discursive acts of persuasion within SUFS through a crowdsourcing process that established what users perceived as truthful and correct (see Jones, 2012). In this way, individual posts or comments were often designed to elicit a response from numerous users whose replies then aggregated to confer general worldviews and arguments. For example, in a document indicative of the employment topic one user asked how many female immigrants coming to Sweden were highly educated, before a series of responses combined to redirect the discussion towards male immigrants and fashion the argument that immigrants are illiterate and unintelligent: "How many of the women who come here are highly educated?" "How many of them can read and write at all is what I want to know" "Most of those who come are men with an average IQ below 85 right?" "Highly-educated is a term that means different things in different cultures."

Within this crowdsourcing process SUFS users regularly used three main argumentation strategies -selective information and pseudo-statistics; truth claims and conspiratorial critiques; and comparisons and alternatives-to justify the nomination, referential, predicational and perspectivation strategies underpinning their anti-immigration and racist discourses. These crowdsourced arguments were directed within the group in a confirmatory manner but also beyond it to those "real Swedes" perceived to be ignorant or cowardly, in a bid to 'empower', 'inform' and convert them. Meanwhile, SUFS users' counter-reactions to the broader public response to their viewpoints as well as their reactions to these arguments within the group itself also relied on mitigation and intensification strategies linked to the denial and (re)definition, deferral and reporting, and persistence, visibility and reach of racism and racist content.

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
sections.header_raw: Argumentation strategies Selective information and pseudo-statistics
sections.header_norm: argumentation strategies selective information and pseudo statistics
paragraphs: In SUFS, anti-immigration and racist discourses were regularly buttressed through the selective reference to, often decontextualized, digital sources of information. Due to Facebook's architecture of embedded hyperlinks and the ease with which digital content can be copied and pasted to the platform, fragments of piecemeal and often spurious information were accumulated to form seemingly comprehensive, one-sided anti-immigration arguments. Mainstream and alternative news stories that supported negative views of immigrants were repeatedly shared while others were twisted to the same ends partly through "context collapse" (see boyd, 2011). Comparative stories featuring "real Swedes" were conspicuously absent. Similarly, academics whose research is critical of Sweden's immigration policy, had the complexity of their findings and politics flattened to more closely match SUFS's dominant views. Mimicking academic conventions, some contributors employed pseudo-statistical argumentation strategies. This was evident in discussions associated with the economy and criminality topics where selective, decontextualized and irrelevant statistical comparisons were used to suggest government overspending on immigration and to emphasize immigrant delinquency. For example, one user wrote:

(…) the sum of the costs of immigration amount to 319,98 billion kronor. This constitutes 33% of the total state budget. Divided by a workforce of 4,892,800 people this becomes a cost of 65,397 kronor per tax payer or 7,3% of Sweden's GDP. Added to this are all the costs that have been placed upon the municipalities for the costs of immigration.

In this way, the discursive power afforded to statistics and numbers by SUFS users because of their assumed ability to convey rationality coalesced with Facebook's emphasis on metrification (see Gerlitz & Helmond, 2013).

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
paragraphs: Information that counters anti-immigration discourses was also shared in SUFS but mostly in order to be discredited as the manipulated creation of the PC establishment. The affordances of social media that resonate with the so-called "post-truth age," where objective facts are less influential than personal beliefs and emotions, were clearly at play here. Thus, users considered the information that supported their opinions as "true facts" whereas the opposite evidenced the mainstream's disregard for veracity. In turn, a news article that accused the group of racism was denigrated as deceitful and its author described as traitorous. At the same time users avoided adding to the article's metrics and advertisement revenue, showing a keen awareness of Facebook's algorithmic agency, by copying and pasting the article's text rather than linking to it. In extreme these truth claims contributed to broader racist conspiracy theories like the Eurabia theory (see Bangstad, 2015). Another more prevalent conspiracy theory was that which suggested The Swedish National Council for Crime Prevention had intentionally adjusted its crime statistics in order to protect immigrants. SUFS users speculated that the council's statistics were inaccurate and that the immigrant share of national crime figures was far higher than suggested.

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
paragraphs: SUFS contributors used comparisons and alternatives to support their anti-immigration stance. For example, within documents characteristic of the international immigration topic, the Hungarian government's resistance to the EU migration policy that requires member states to adopt refugee quotas was celebrated. Some argued this policy was punishment for Europe's colonial past and nostalgically reframed and simplified Sweden and Hungary's pasts in order to downplay their colonial guilt and create a sense of injustice around the expectation that Sweden should shoulder colonialism's consequences. Users enviously suggested that the Swedish prime minister should emulate his Hungarian counterpart. Others celebrated American President Donald Trump's travel ban as an example of "care for his fellow citizens." Echoing these sentiments one contributor suggested a detailed alternative immigration policy founded on the principle of "repatriation" rather than integration with various demands for change including the non-conditional deportation of unidentifiable, illegal and criminal immigrants. sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
paragraphs: The denial and (re)definition of racism

As is common within covert racist discourse, the denial and (re)definition of racism was evident within SUFS as indicated by its characterization of a topic. Confirming established precedents, many users emphasized the "debatability of racism" by arguing that their status as truth-tellers, victims and realists precluded their consideration as racists (Titley, 2016;Van Dijk, 1992). Instead users claimed that immigrants are more racist towards "real Swedes" than the reverse. These forms of racism denial bridged into racism's redefinition and attempts to erode the discursive power of racism accusations. In one discussion, racism was equated to realism, encouraging users to ironically and sarcastically declare, on the basis of this equation, their proud status as racists. Elsewhere, tautological and antischolarly lines of reasoning reverted to narrow biologically-based definitions of overt racism in order to exclude and counter broader definitions of covert cultural and raceless racism. Users deflected accusations of racism by arguing that immigrants cannot suffer racism because they are not a single race (see Goldberg, 2015). "Saying that 'all immigrants are criminal' is not racist. To distinguish between races is racism. To say that 'all immigrants are criminals' is to have preconceived opinions about a particular group with different origins." In some SUFS users' views, those that accused them of racism were the true racists because their accusations denied the racial equality of humankind (see Valluvan, 2016). "If you say the word racist that must mean you believe people belong to different races! I think THAT is racist!" These mitigation strategies functioned partly by creating noise (Titley, 2016) and were aided by Facebook's community standards vague definition of race-based hate.

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
sections.header_raw: The deferral and reporting of racism
sections.header_norm: the deferral and reporting of racism
paragraphs: When it comes to combating racism, Facebook relies on the deferral of its liability through chains of transference to group admins and moderators, and then the users behind content (Ben-David & Matamoros-Fernández, 2016). This has been evidenced by the conviction of a number of SUFS users for hate crimes based on their posts in the group (Herkel, 2018). Awareness of these dynamics is illustrated by SUFS's explicit rules against racism and by discussions regarding how to use words strategically so to avoid losing credibility, attracting accusations of racism, and "playing into the hands of censorship." In other words, some users acknowledged that overtly racist content could lead to SUFS's closure. The platform's crowdsourcing capacity can further insulate individual users from liability as racist discourses and arguments are constructed or insinuated through the accumulation of posts and comments which on an individual basis appear more innocuous. Facebook, thus not only provides an architecture for the networked circulation and accumulation of covert racism, but also inadvertently encourages those with racist views to perfect their means of expressing them covertly. The company also defers much of the responsibility for "flagging" racist content to group admins and users through an architecture that allows the reporting of posts, comments and replies (Crawford & Gillespie, 2016). The extent to which SUFS serves as an enclave of anti-immigration opinion likely restricts its own contributors from reporting content but its simultaneous function as a gateway also allows this content to be reported by a broader population of Facebook users. It is uncertain how such reports are resolved but SUFS admins did remove and/or block users responsible for some of the most overtly racist content posted to the group.

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
sections.header_raw: The persistence, visibility and reach of racism
sections.header_norm: the persistence visibility and reach of racism
paragraphs: While these users were removed or blocked from SUFS, many of their racist contributions remained in the group, creating digital residues of racism. Whether these were solely the result of the options provided by Facebook when removing or blocking users, which allow the removal of a user's posts and comments only for the preceding seven days, or whether they were also a consequence of the admins' tactical use of these options is hard to establish. Regardless, the persistence that Facebook can afford racist content lends it discursive permanence with consequences for racism's normalization and intensification. The visibility and spreadability that Facebook affords UGC has similar consequences (boyd, 2011). Facebook's "like" and "reaction" buttons magnify certain discourses and although the posts discussed here were not the most popular in the group they still averaged 90 reactions eachmetrics that added endorsement, relevance, legitimacy and influence to their content and ensuing discussion (see Gerlitz & Helmond, 2013;Matamoros-Fernández, 2017). The visibility of covertly racist content was also magnified by users who, as the alternative immigration policy example illustrates, took advantage of the ease with which digital platforms allow the duplication of content. The repetition of content increased its reach within but also beyond SUFS as it appeared in the individual feeds of the group's members. Facebook's "share" function, which was used on average nine times for each of the 54 sampled posts, further contributed to the spread of covert and overt racist discourses. Posts shared to private Facebook profiles and other groups act as vehicles for the potential intensification and normalization of racist content indicating again SUFS's role as both an enclave in which racism can be expressed and a gateway through which it can pass to become more mainstreamed.

sections.paper_id: Merrill and Åkerlund - 2018 - Standing Up for Sweden The Racist Discourses, Architectures and Affordances of an Anti-Immigration
paragraphs: In SUFS, immigration has been discussed most explicitly in relation to 10 topics and these discussions have, in part, relied on long-established, pre-digital anti-immigrant discourses regularly supported by covert, and occasionally overt forms of racism. Users of the group most often identified as "real Swedes" who, unlike the majority of Sweden's population, know and are brave enough to share "the truth" about immigration. Some adhered to newer anti-immigrant and racist discourses linked to conspiracy theories with historical lineages more closely aligned to the digital platforms through which they have spread. Their discursive repertoire, in turn, reveals that the Sweden that those in SUFS tended to "stand up for" is primarily and nostalgically white, male-dominated and anti-elitist and in these states perceived to be, somewhat counterintuitively, vulnerable and fragile. The racist discourse evident in SUFS is not entirely new but the use of Facebook enables it normalization and mainstreaming in new ways. The platform's connective architectures allowed covert and overt forms of racism to accumulate, forming different cybertypes that underpinned forms of digital role-play among users who cast themselves as truth-tellers, victims and realists. These strategies of identification were supported by strategies of persuasion manifested partly through crowdsourcing processes. The accretive collection of selective and piecemeal information from other digital sources served to argue for and reinforce commonly-held truth claims and conspiratorial critiques while also supplying users with a suite of fanciful alternatives to Sweden's current immigration policy. Similar processes supported the counter-reactions of SUFS contributors against accusations of racism through digitally-enabled forms of racism denial and (re)definition while ultimately the regulatory infrastructure of Facebook deferred retributory action to single individuals. Meanwhile weaknesses in Facebook's system of reporting allowed residual racist content to persist digitally, even if action was taken against offending individuals. Not only the persistence but also the visibility and reach that Facebook can afford racist discourse contributed to the intensification and normalization of racism. Given these conditions SUFS was able to serve as both an enclave of anti-immigration discourse that bred covert cultural and postracial racism and, simultaneously, a gateway that allowed that racism's greater mainstreaming.

Establishing who should be responsible for closing that gateway, or indeed whether it should be closed, is problematic for a number of reasons that have resonance beyond the case of SUFS. The banning or removal of entire Facebook groups tends to activate broader debates about freedom of speech, which often hinge on the tension between a digital libertarian ethos and a desire to define what is morally acceptable to write or share online (see Back, 2002). These tensions map onto the disjuncture between American and European understandings of free speech with the former being more absolutist than the latter (Daniels, 2012). In this respect it is worth acknowledging Facebook's national origins and in turn the company's reluctance to be accused of censorship, even if much of the content that it proscribes might be argued to be protected by the First Amendment (see Heins, 2014). In short, Facebook's removal of groups can probably only be expected in the most extreme cases and is unlikely for a group like SUFS. Indeed, given SUFS's overall focus on freedom of speech, its outright and complete censorship would likely only strengthen the resolve of its contributors and be used to buttress their claims. Where there has been precedent for such actions in some cases this has only followed the retributory efforts against Facebook groups by third parties (see Oboler, 2008). Those opposing the use of Facebook to spread racism have also enjoyed success by adopting crowdsourced forms of activism that exploit the platform's reporting functions (see Farkas & Neumayer, 2017). This suggests that countering the normalization and mainstreaming of racism in Facebook groups like SUFS might be best achieved by focusing on what passes through the gateway rather than the gateway itself. Neutralizing racist discourse through its greater reporting, however, will only be viable if Facebook also develops functions in its platform that allow admins and moderators to review, and if need be, remove not only reported content and those responsible for it, but all offending content originating from that user in a particular group. This could prevent the creation of residual racist content with the ability to maintain discursive influence.

Such measures might place further pressure on group admins and moderators, and with extremely large groups, as with SUFS, it may be unrealistic to expect what is usually a small number of volunteers to regulate such quantities of content and remove all traces of racism. At its peak SUFS had just a handful of admins and moderators. Their role, and particularly that of SUFS's creator, in curating the group's discussions has been rather ambivalent but their responsibility should not be underplayed. This is particularly so given that SUFS has attracted individuals associated with explicitly racist organizations and social movements, and because attempts have been made to use the group as a means to mobilize people to take part in more radical forms of street activism. The influence of the group's creator was evidenced again in January 2018 when they closed the group, publicly citing their and the group's other admins and moderators' persistent exposure to hate as the reason behind their decision. While this may have been true, it is hard to ignore how such claims mirrored the group's common perspectivation strategy of victimhood. More than that, it reminds us that SUFS's gateway goes both ways. With the group reopened by its creator just five days later, perhaps herein lies the key to combatting its and similar groups' role in the promulgation and mainstreaming of racism. Maybe it is time that a greater number of Facebook users enter SUFS's enclave and make clear their negative view of both overt and covert racist discourse, not through reactionary hate but recourse to the reporting functions provided by Facebook, functions which in time and through continued use might be refined for the task of identifying and combatting racism in all its forms. Further research will show whether a user-led approach such as this can be effective and whether it will motivate Facebook, as a social media company, to improve its platform or merely efface its responsibility in terms of both perpetuating and tackling the problem of racism. While this article has highlighted how the architecture of Facebook affords racism within a broader CDA approach, future research would do well to target the role of social media architectures and affordances in the production and interpretation of digital hate and racist discourse even more precisely. Such efforts should not be restricted to Facebook, not least because the problems associated with its architectures and affordances discussed here find parallels across many other social media companies and platforms.

While this article has highlighted how the architecture of Facebook affords racism within a broader CDA approach, future research would do well to target the role of social media architectures and affordances in the production and interpretation of digital hate and racist discourse even more precisely. Such efforts should not be restricted to Facebook, not least because the problems associated with its architectures and affordances discussed here find parallels across many other social media companies and platforms.

In SUFS, immigration has been discussed most explicitly in relation to 10 topics and these discussions have, in part, relied on long-established, pre-digital anti-immigrant discourses regularly supported by covert, and occasionally overt forms of racism. Users of the group most often identified as "real Swedes" who, unlike the majority of Sweden's population, know and are brave enough to share "the truth" about immigration. Some adhered to newer anti-immigrant and racist discourses linked to conspiracy theories with historical lineages more closely aligned to the digital platforms through which they have spread. Their discursive repertoire, in turn, reveals that the Sweden that those in SUFS tended to "stand up for" is primarily and nostalgically white, male-dominated and anti-elitist and in these states perceived to be, somewhat counterintuitively, vulnerable and fragile. The racist discourse evident in SUFS is not entirely new but the use of Facebook enables it normalization and mainstreaming in new ways. The platform's connective architectures allowed covert and overt forms of racism to accumulate, forming different cybertypes that underpinned forms of digital role-play among users who cast themselves as truth-tellers, victims and realists. These strategies of identification were supported by strategies of persuasion manifested partly through crowdsourcing processes. The accretive collection of selective and piecemeal information from other digital sources served to argue for and reinforce commonly-held truth claims and conspiratorial critiques while also supplying users with a suite of fanciful alternatives to Sweden's current immigration policy. Similar processes supported the counter-reactions of SUFS contributors against accusations of racism through digitally-enabled forms of racism denial and (re)definition while ultimately the regulatory infrastructure of Facebook deferred retributory action to single individuals. Meanwhile weaknesses in Facebook's system of reporting allowed residual racist content to persist digitally, even if action was taken against offending individuals. Not only the persistence but also the visibility and reach that Facebook can afford racist discourse contributed to the intensification and normalization of racism. Given these conditions SUFS was able to serve as both an enclave of anti-immigration discourse that bred covert cultural and postracial racism and, simultaneously, a gateway that allowed that racism's greater mainstreaming.

Establishing who should be responsible for closing that gateway, or indeed whether it should be closed, is problematic for a number of reasons that have resonance beyond the case of SUFS. The banning or removal of entire Facebook groups tends to activate broader debates about freedom of speech, which often hinge on the tension between a digital libertarian ethos and a desire to define what is morally acceptable to write or share online (see Back, 2002). These tensions map onto the disjuncture between American and European understandings of free speech with the former being more absolutist than the latter (Daniels, 2012). In this respect it is worth acknowledging Facebook's national origins and in turn the company's reluctance to be accused of censorship, even if much of the content that it proscribes might be argued to be protected by the First Amendment (see Heins, 2014). In short, Facebook's removal of groups can probably only be expected in the most extreme cases and is unlikely for a group like SUFS. Indeed, given SUFS's overall focus on freedom of speech, its outright and complete censorship would likely only strengthen the resolve of its contributors and be used to buttress their claims. Where there has been precedent for such actions in some cases this has only followed the retributory efforts against Facebook groups by third parties (see Oboler, 2008). Those opposing the use of Facebook to spread racism have also enjoyed success by adopting crowdsourced forms of activism that exploit the platform's reporting functions (see Farkas & Neumayer, 2017). This suggests that countering the normalization and mainstreaming of racism in Facebook groups like SUFS might be best achieved by focusing on what passes through the gateway rather than the gateway itself. Neutralizing racist discourse through its greater reporting, however, will only be viable if Facebook also develops functions in its platform that allow admins and moderators to review, and if need be, remove not only reported content and those responsible for it, but all offending content originating from that user in a particular group. This could prevent the creation of residual racist content with the ability to maintain discursive influence.

Such measures might place further pressure on group admins and moderators, and with extremely large groups, as with SUFS, it may be unrealistic to expect what is usually a small number of volunteers to regulate such quantities of content and remove all traces of racism. At its peak SUFS had just a handful of admins and moderators. Their role, and particularly that of SUFS's creator, in curating the group's discussions has been rather ambivalent but their responsibility should not be underplayed. This is particularly so given that SUFS has attracted individuals associated with explicitly racist organizations and social movements, and because attempts have been made to use the group as a means to mobilize people to take part in more radical forms of street activism. The influence of the group's creator was evidenced again in January 2018 when they closed the group, publicly citing their and the group's other admins and moderators' persistent exposure to hate as the reason behind their decision. While this may have been true, it is hard to ignore how such claims mirrored the group's common perspectivation strategy of victimhood. More than that, it reminds us that SUFS's gateway goes both ways. With the group reopened by its creator just five days later, perhaps herein lies the key to combatting its and similar groups' role in the promulgation and mainstreaming of racism. Maybe it is time that a greater number of Facebook users enter SUFS's enclave and make clear their negative view of both overt and covert racist discourse, not through reactionary hate but recourse to the reporting functions provided by Facebook, functions which in time and through continued use might be refined for the task of identifying and combatting racism in all its forms. Further research will show whether a user-led approach such as this can be effective and whether it will motivate Facebook, as a social media company, to improve its platform or merely efface its responsibility in terms of both perpetuating and tackling the problem of racism. While this article has highlighted how the architecture of Facebook affords racism within a broader CDA approach, future research would do well to target the role of social media architectures and affordances in the production and interpretation of digital hate and racist discourse even more precisely. Such efforts should not be restricted to Facebook, not least because the problems associated with its architectures and affordances discussed here find parallels across many other social media companies and platforms.
